canonical_examples.analysis: Spence's job-market model treats education as a signal of worker type under asymmetric information. Employers cannot directly observe productivity before hiring, but workers know more about their own ability. If education is less costly for high-productivity workers than for low-productivity workers, a degree can separate types even if schooling does not directly raise productivity. Employers rationally pay more to degree holders because the costly signal changes beliefs about type. The analysis asks whether the education threshold is incentive-compatible: high-productivity workers must prefer acquiring the signal and low-productivity workers must prefer not to mimic.
canonical_examples.problem: Michael Spence's job-market signaling model asks why employers pay more for workers with educational credentials when productivity is not directly observable.

canonical_examples.analysis: Zahavi's handicap principle explains extravagant traits such as the peacock's tail as costly signals of fitness. A large tail is a survival burden, so it cannot be cheaply faked by low-quality males; only individuals with enough underlying fitness can afford the handicap. The signal is credible because its cost is differentially bearable by high-quality senders. The framework therefore interprets the trait not as inefficient ornament alone but as an equilibrium communication system between signaller and receiver shaped by selection.
canonical_examples.problem: Amotz Zahavi used conspicuous animal displays, classically the peacock's tail, to argue that costly handicaps can honestly signal fitness.

core_claims: A signal is credible only when its cost, risk, or constraint differs by type so that lower-quality senders cannot profitably mimic higher-quality senders.
Observable actions can transmit private information even when the action has little direct productive value.
Separating equilibria require incentive compatibility for both sides: high types must prefer sending the signal and low types must prefer not to imitate.
Cheap talk is unreliable when sender and receiver incentives diverge, but can work when interests are aligned or messages are externally disciplined.
Wasteful expenditure can be informationally productive if it prevents pooling and allows receivers to condition their behavior on type.
Signal value is equilibrium-dependent: once a signal becomes too cheap, too common, or easy to counterfeit, it stops distinguishing types.
Receivers are active participants in signaling systems because their response rules determine whether sending the signal pays.
description: Signaling Theory explains how informed actors credibly communicate hidden quality, type, intention, or fitness through observable signals whose costs make imitation unattractive for lower-quality senders.
failure_modes: It can mistake every costly behavior for communication, ignoring direct utility, coercion, habit, institutional requirements, or nonstrategic causes.
Practitioners often infer honesty from cost alone, but a costly signal is credible only if cost differs by type and receivers respond in equilibrium.
The framework can rationalize wasteful status competition as useful sorting while neglecting distributive harm and arms-race dynamics.
Signals can become obsolete through grade inflation, credential inflation, counterfeiting, or changed receiver beliefs, making old equilibria misleading.
The characteristic practitioner error is treating the signal as proof of the underlying quality rather than probabilistic evidence shaped by selection and incentives.

key_vocabulary.Cheap talk: Costless or nearly costless communication. It is generally not credible when sender and receiver interests conflict, though it may be informative under aligned interests or repeated interaction.
key_vocabulary.Costly signal: An observable action, trait, expenditure, or commitment that is expensive enough to make imitation unattractive for lower-quality or differently motivated types.
key_vocabulary.Differential cost: The condition that a signal costs less, yields more, or is more survivable for one type than another, allowing the signal to separate types.
key_vocabulary.Handicap principle: Zahavi's claim that reliable biological signals can be honest because they impose a survival or reproductive handicap that only high-quality individuals can bear.
key_vocabulary.Pooling equilibrium: An outcome in which different types send the same signal or no distinguishing signal, so receivers cannot infer type.
key_vocabulary.Receiver: The actor who observes the signal and updates behavior, such as an employer, mate, investor, rival, or audience.
key_vocabulary.Separating equilibrium: An outcome in which different types choose different observable signals because the incentive constraints make mimicry unprofitable.
key_vocabulary.Signaller: The informed actor whose type, quality, intention, or state is partly hidden and who may take observable action to influence receivers.
key_vocabulary.Spence signal: A signal, such as education in Spence's model, whose value comes from sorting types under asymmetric information rather than necessarily producing the quality it indicates.
key_vocabulary.Strategic mimicry: Attempted imitation of a high-type signal by a lower type; credible signaling requires that mimicry not be worth its cost.

name: Signaling Theory | costly signals as credibility (Spence, Zahavi handicap principle)
operative_procedure: Identify the hidden attribute: specify the quality, type, intention, fitness, reliability, or commitment that receivers cannot directly observe.
Identify signallers and receivers: state who knows the private information, who observes the signal, and what response the sender wants to induce.
List candidate signals: include credentials, prices, warranties, sacrifices, displays, commitments, rituals, investments, delays, or refusals.
Test observability and manipulability: ask whether receivers can see the signal, whether it can be verified, and how easily it can be counterfeited.
Check differential cost by type: determine whether the signal is cheaper, more valuable, less risky, or more survivable for the type it is supposed to reveal.
Write the incentive constraints: verify that high types prefer to signal and low types prefer not to mimic, given receiver responses.
Classify the equilibrium: decide whether the situation pools types, separates types, partially pools, or cycles through escalation and counterfeiting.
Evaluate signal efficiency: distinguish informational value from direct productive value, and identify waste, exclusion, arms races, or credential inflation.
Recommend signal design or interpretation: strengthen hard-to-fake commitments, add verification, change receiver response rules, or discount signals that fail differential-cost tests.

ancestors: Information Asymmetry | Akerlof lemons, adverse selection, moral hazard
composes_with: Mechanism Design | reverse game theory: design rules so selfish play yields desired outcomes
related: Information Asymmetry | Akerlof lemons, adverse selection, moral hazard
Goodhart's Law | when a measure becomes a target it ceases to be a good measure

scope_and_limits: Signaling Theory is strongest where hidden types and observable but costly actions shape beliefs: labor credentials, warranties, brands, biological displays, diplomacy, status goods, startup financing, and online trust. It is weaker where communication is cheap but interests are aligned, where direct production effects dominate signaling, or where culture and identity determine meaning more than incentive-compatible sorting.
source_summary: Authored from model knowledge of Spence, Zahavi, information economics, evolutionary signaling, and costly signaling literature, without fetched source text.

source_texts.title: Amotz Zahavi, 'Mate Selection: A Selection for a Handicap' (1975)

when_to_use: one actor knows their own quality, type, intention, commitment, or fitness better than observers do
observers rely on credentials, rituals, prices, brands, sacrifices, displays, warranties, or commitments as evidence
cheap verbal claims are distrusted because the sender benefits if receivers believe them
a costly or apparently wasteful behavior may function by making imitation too expensive for lower-quality actors
a market or social field shows credential inflation, status competition, counterfeiting, or erosion of trust in indicators
